raw_case:
Malignant STK11 adnexal tumor harboring a somatic mutation in a woman previously diagnosed with mesothelioma, a case report


Highlights

•The novel STK11 adnexal tumor may not be associated with Peutz-Jeghers syndrome.•Diagnosis of the STK11 adnexal tumor requires a high index of suspicion given its immunohistochemical and pathologic similarity to mesothelioma.•Further research is necessary to identify and develop evidence-based treatment strategies for these unique tumors.STK11 germline pathogenic variants are typically associated with Peutz-Jeghers syndrome, an autosomal dominant disease characterized by hamartomatous polyps in the gastrointestinal tract, hyperpigmented patches, and increased risk of stomach, colorectal, small bowel, and breast cancers (Beggs et al., 2010). Mutations in this gene have also been identified in skin, pancreatic, testicular, and stromal ovarian cancer (Fagerberg et al., 2014). To date, there have been less than 30 cases of ovarian cancer reported associated with mutated STK11 (Bennett et al., 2021). In this report, we discuss a rare case of a STK11 adnexal tumor in a 39-year-old woman previously diagnosed with malignant mesothelioma. After 33 months with no evidence of disease following cytoreductive surgery with HIPEC and adjuvant chemotherapy, a new retroperitoneal lesion was noted on imaging. After resection, molecular testing indicated an STK11 mutation, and histology was consistent with an STK11 adnexal tumor. A high index of suspicion is required to make the diagnosis of STK11 adnexal tumor due to its non-distinct pathology and IHC staining. Due to the rarity of this neoplasm, analysis of current and future cases of the STK11 adnexal tumor is necessary to understand its pathogenesis, genetic mutational analysis, clinical course, and best treatment options.1

Introduction

Peutz-Jeghers syndrome (PJS) is an autosomal dominant disease characterized by hamartomatous polyps, hyperpigmented patches, and increased risk of stomach, colorectal, small bowel, and breast cancers (Beggs et al., 2010). It is caused by a germline loss of heterozygosity (LOH) mutation in the STK11 gene, a serine threonine kinase located on chromosome 19, previously known as LKB1 (Liver Kinase B1), which functions as a tumor suppressor on the AMP-protein kinase pathway (Daniell et al., 2018). Mutations in STK11 have been linked to an increased risk of stomach, colorectal, small bowel, breast, pancreatic, testicular, stromal ovarian, and skin cancers (Fagerberg et al., 2014). In 1970, the unique entity, sex cord tumor with annular tubules (SCTAT), was identified in the ovaries of multiple women with PJS (Scully, 1970). SCTAT is largely a benign variant of a sex cord stromal ovarian tumor, described to have an intermediate morphology between a granulosa cell and Sertoli cell tumor (Scully, 1970). However, about 20 % of SCTAT tumors harbor the potential to undergo malignant transformation (Beggs et al., 2010).STK11 adnexal tumors are considered distinct from the previously defined SCTAT tumors that are associated with PJS and are a recently defined entity. STK11 adnexal tumors harbor an STK11 mutation and are uncommon, with less than 30 cases reported in the literature (Bennett et al., 2021). While these tumors harbor LOH events as in other tumors identified in PJS, not all ovarian neoplasms with STK11 mutations accompany a concurrent diagnosis of PJS. Review of these cases by Bennett et al. failed to identify gross and microscopic pathology that make the STK11 adnexal tumors unique. They are associated with a broad range of epithelial, sex cord stromal, and mesothelial immunohistochemistry (IHC) markers. Unlike SCTAT, STK11 adnexal tumors are highly malignant, with local infiltration and high rates of recurrence (Bennett et al., 2021 Aug, Schultz et al., 2016 Oct). In some cases, an STK11 adnexal tumor and SCTAT existed in the same patient and demonstrated morphologically distinct features from each other (Bennett et al., 2021). Additionally, STK11 adnexal tumors share many IHC markers consistent with malignant peritoneal mesothelioma (MPM) such as WT1 and calretinin; however, their growth pattern tends to be a discrete mass rather than plaque-like growth (Bennett et al., 2021). The STK11 adnexal tumor appears to be a novel and rare entity, which makes diagnosis challenging and has no standard of care treatment recommendation.This case report reviews a 39-year-old female with an STK11 adnexal tumor following a presumed diagnosis of MPM. Here we present her diagnostic workup and treatment regimen for a rare tumor without standard of care guidelines.2

Case

A 39-year-old woman presented with 6 months of worsening right upper quadrant pain, gastrointestinal distress, decreased appetite and early satiety after cholecystectomy three years prior. She was treated for presumed pancreatitis. Computed tomography (CT) of the abdomen revealed an incidental 3.7 × 1.4 cm subcapsular liver lesion. Follow up CT indicated marginal growth of the enhancing mass to 4.9 × 1.9 cm, with magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) suggesting a hemangioma versus hepatocellular carcinoma. Cancer antigens 125 (CA-125) and 19-9 (CA 19-9), as well as alpha fetoprotein (AFP) measurements were all within normal limits. CT guided biopsy of the liver mass revealed atypical epithelioid pathology, potentially representing a MPM.Given the uncertainty of her diagnosis, she underwent local excision of the lesion for diagnostic and therapeutic purposes. Diagnostic laparoscopy revealed a peritoneal surface mass over the right dome of the liver with adhesions and involvement of the right diaphragm, multiple peritoneal nodules on the right diaphragm, a solitary peritoneal nodule in the small-bowel mesentery, multiple peritoneal nodules on the tip of the omentum, which was adherent in the vicinity of the right ovary but not directly attached, and a small right ovarian simple cyst. Pathology of the omental nodule tumor showed anastomosing cords and papillae, gland-like areas, and areas of sieve-like architecture (Fig. 1 a-b). The sample stained positive for several markers, including D2-40, calretinin, and WT1. It was favored to be an atypical epithelioid pathology and was sent for expert consultation, after which the diagnosis of MPM was supported.The patient underwent cytoreductive surgery with hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy (HIPEC) with mitomycin C. Cytoreductive surgery included stripping of the right diaphragm, small bowel mesentery and pelvic sidewall, partial resection of the segment 8 liver lesion, omentectomy, appendectomy, total abdominal hysterectomy, and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. Her peritoneal cancer index was <10. Pathology was consistent with MPM, pathologic stage pT4c, pN0, cM0. Her gynecologic organs had no pathologic abnormality on final pathology. Next generation sequencing (NGS) results revealed mutations in ARID1A, EP300 and STK11 (splice site 375-1delC) as well as a low tumor mutational burden (1 Muts/Mb) and microsatellite stability. After recovering from surgery, she was treated with adjuvant chemotherapy consisting of 3 cycles of cisplatin 75 mg/m2 and pemetrexed 500 mg/m2.She had no evidence of disease and remained asymptomatic for 33 months before a 1.1 cm retroperitoneal nodule was noted on surveillance imaging. Surgical pathology from CT guided biopsy of this mass was non-diagnostic, therefore she underwent laparoscopic surgical excision. At the time of surgery, the only site of obvious disease was a 2-cm mesenteric mass in the sigmoid colon mesentery which was completely resected. On pathology, the tumor was morphologically similar to the previous specimen, with similar architecture and relatively bland, monotonous cytologic features (Fig. 1 c-d); however, MPM was not favored. The IHC stains resulted in tumor positivity for CD10, calretinin, WT-1, and patchy positivity for estrogen receptors (ER) (Fig. 2 a-d). Tumor cells were negative for PAX8, B72.3, D2-40, Glypican-3, SALL-4, GATA3, inhibin, androgen receptor, CK7, and progesterone receptor. Pathology was again sent for expert consultation where the diagnosis of a rare STK11 adnexal tumor was favored, given her previous specimen with an STK11 mutation and the above staining pattern. NGS of her recurrent tumor revealed the same STK11 mutation and an ARID1A deletion, but lacked the EP300 mutation, although testing was completed by a different panel. The MI Genomic Prevalence Score (MI GPSai) was 96 % ovarian origin. Overall, her diagnosis favored an STK11 adnexal tumor rather than recurrent MPM, with a potential misclassification of the original tumor.Given the association of STK11 with PJS, it was recommended that this patient undergo genetic testing, which did not identify any pathogenic germline mutations. Postoperatively, a positron emission tomography (PET)/CT scan revealed a liver subcapsular lesion with follow up MRI concerning for metastatic implant. The patient received 6 cycles of adjuvant carboplatin AUC 5 and paclitaxel 135 mg/m2, given the excellent response of her primary tumor to platinum-based chemotherapy. Repeat PET/CT scan after her 6th cycle revealed resolution of the segment 8 subcapsular focal hypermetabolic activity compared to her pretreatment scan. She is currently in surveillance and has no evidence of disease at 4 months after her final treatment dose as of the time this case report was submitted.3

Discussion

In this report, we review a rare case of STK11 adnexal tumor, of which there are less than 30 cases reported in the literature (Bennett et al., 2021). This patient was originally diagnosed with MPM; however, in retrospect, she likely had an STK11 adnexal tumor. STK11 pathogenic variants are classically associated with PJS. Interestingly, our patient does not harbor a germline mutation in STK11, nor does she have any clinical signs consistent with PJS. The non-specific markers and non-descript pathologic appearance of STK11 adnexal tumors contribute to the difficulty in diagnosis based on histology alone. STK11 tumors are often misclassified as female adnexal tumor of probably wolffian origin (FATWO), MPM, sex cord stromal tumors, or Mullerian adenocarcinomas (7). This patient’s peritoneal spread of cancer, absence of distinct ovarian mass, and positive staining for mesothelial markers led to a misdiagnosis of MPM; however it is notable that STK11 adnexal tumors are often paratubal in origin, as in our patient (Bennett et al., 2021). Treatment of her primary cancer with platinum-based chemotherapy resulted in 33 months of progression-free survival. This timeline is consistent with previous reports of STK11 adnexal tumors, where mean time to recurrence is 29 months after initial diagnosis (Bennett et al., 2021).Analysis of her recurrent cancer identified the same STK11 and ARID1A mutations as her primary cancer, although it lacked the EP300 mutation. Notably, the NGS of her primary and secondary malignancies were done by different companies, therefore may not be directly comparable. ARID1A and EP300 mutations have not been previously reported with STK11 adnexal tumors. ARID1A mutations are well known to be associated with ovarian endometrioid an ovarian clear cell carcinomas as well as endometrial cancer, hepatocellular carcinoma, and other gastrointestinal cancers. They are thought to be due to induction of the Wnt and PI3K/AKT pathways (Xu and Tang, 2021). Interestingly, mutations in ARID1A were not seen in analysis of known STK11 tumors by Bennett et al (2021). EP300 mutations have been identified in some epithelial cancers, as well as hematological malignancies and function as a tumor-suppressor gene, but not in any known STK11 adnexal tumors Gayther et al., 2000.A high index of suspicion is required to make the diagnosis of STK11 adnexal tumor due to its non-distinct pathology and IHC staining. STK11 neoplasms were not well described until 2021, and prior to this many were diagnosed as MPMs in light of their location on mesothelial-lined surfaces as well as propensity to stain for mesothelial markers (Bennett et al., 2021). This patient’s original tumor did not involve the adnexa and she had completely benign gynecologic organ on resection, which likely added to her confusing pathologic diagnosis. During her primary resection, tumor implants were near the right ovary but not directly attached, raising the possibility of a primary paratubal location of her tumor.Treatment of reported cases of the STK11 adnexal tumor varies. Some have been treated with surgery followed by platinum-based chemotherapy, with maintained treatment response (Sharma et al., 2023). Others were treated with mTOR inhibitor and endocrine therapy with partial response (Estevez-Diz et al., 2021). In a review of current identified STK11 adnexal tumors by Bennett et al., 47 % of tumors were treated with adjuvant therapy, all aside from one including chemotherapy. Recurrence occurred in 80 % of cases available for follow-up at a mean time of 29 months, most commonly in the omentum, abdominal wall, peritoneum, or pelvis (Bennett et al., 2021). Analysis of current and future cases of the STK11 adnexal tumor is required to form a pattern of initial presentation, disease progression, treatment response, immune marker profile, pathologic appearance, and genetic mutational analysis to identify possible trends, helpful early identification markers, and create evidence-based treatment guidelines.4

Conclusion

The STK11 adnexal tumor is a rare entity that may or may not be associated with PJS. Given its histological and immunohistochemical similarity to malignant mesothelioma, a high level of suspicion is required for accurate diagnosis. Treatment with platinum-based chemotherapy may be effective in treating the tumor burden in these patients.

CRediT authorship contribution statement

Christina R. Rutherford: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Taylor A. Rives: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Conceptualization. Dava W. Piecoro: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Charles S. Dietrich: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Conceptualization.

Declaration of competing interest

The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 39-year-old woman  
- **Chief Complaint:** 6 months of worsening right upper quadrant pain, gastrointestinal distress, decreased appetite, and early satiety.  
- **History of Present Illness:** Symptom onset was 6 months prior to presentation. A subcapsular liver lesion was noted after imaging. It was initially thought to represent malignant peritoneal mesothelioma (MPM). Surgical intervention and chemotherapy resulted in a stable disease course for 33 months before recurrence.  
- **Past Medical History:** History of cytoreductive surgery with hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy and adjuvant chemotherapy for presumed MPM. Previous cholecystectomy performed three years earlier.  
- **Personal History:** None reported.  
- **Family History:** None reported.  
- **Physical Examination:** Not explicitly described in the report.  
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - **Imaging:** Subcapsular liver lesion noted with follow-up imaging showing growth. MRI and PET/CT scans showed additional lesions suggestive of metastasis.  
  - **Biopsies:** Initial CT-guided biopsies were inconclusive. Surgical pathology on resection demonstrated atypical epithelioid pathology with IHC positivity for markers like D2-40, calretinin, and WT1. Follow-up pathological workup indicated a rare STK11 adnexal tumor with associated mutations (STK11, ARID1A, EP300).  
  - **Genetic Testing:** Negative for germline mutations associated with Peutz-Jeghers Syndrome.  

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Diagnosis of Malignant Peritoneal Mesothelioma (MPM):**  
   - **Rationale for Inclusion:** Imaging findings of peritoneal lesions, IHC staining for mesothelial markers (e.g., WT1, calretinin), and typical morphology suggested MPM.  
   - **Reason for Exclusion:** Retrospective analysis and molecular testing revealed an STK11 mutation and staining patterns inconsistent with classic MPM. Coupled with the tumor's growth pattern and recurrence presentation, the original diagnosis was re-evaluated.  

2. **Consideration of STK11 Adnexal Tumor:**  
   - **Rationale for Inclusion:** Identification of the somatic STK11 mutation in the resected tissues and pathology findings on the recurrent tumor aligned with features of an STK11 adnexal tumor. Expert consultation confirmed the diagnosis.  
   - **Reason for Confirmation:** Consistent genetic mutations (STK11 and ARID1A) noted in both primary and recurrent tumors. Immunohistochemical staining was compatible with this rare tumor type.  

3. **Exclusion of Peutz-Jeghers Syndrome (PJS)-Related Neoplasms:**  
   - **Rationale for Consideration:** STK11 mutations are commonly linked to PJS-related cancers; hence, PJS was entertained.  
   - **Reason for Exclusion:** Genetic testing for germline mutations of the STK11 gene was negative, and there were no clinical features of PJS.  

4. **Exclusion of Other Gynecological Neoplasms (e.g., Mullerian adenocarcinomas, sex cord stromal tumors):**  
   - **Reason for Exclusion:** Tumor markers (e.g., PAX8, glypican-3, inhibin) were negative, which helped rule out these alternative diagnoses.  

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
The final diagnosis is a **rare STK11 adnexal tumor**, misdiagnosed initially as malignant peritoneal mesothelioma (MPM). This diagnosis was established via pathology review, molecular testing (identifying STK11 and ARID1A mutations), and expert pathologic consultation confirming its distinct immunohistochemical and genetic characteristics.

---

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> **Analyze the Chief Complaint and History of Present Illness:** The patient presents with right upper quadrant pain, gastrointestinal distress, decreased appetite, and early satiety for 6 months.  A liver lesion was initially noted and thought to be malignant peritoneal mesothelioma (MPM). This initial suspicion of MPM is crucial as it directed the initial treatment.

<step 2> **Evaluate Past Medical History:** The patient underwent cytoreductive surgery with HIPEC and adjuvant chemotherapy for presumed MPM.  The disease was stable for 33 months, which is a significant duration for MPM, but recurrence occurred.  A previous cholecystectomy is noted, but less relevant to the current oncological diagnosis.

<step 3> **Consider Ancillary Tests - Imaging:** Imaging revealed a subcapsular liver lesion, which grew on follow-up. MRI and PET/CT scans showed additional lesions suggestive of metastasis. This indicates disease progression and spread beyond the initial liver lesion.

<step 4> **Analyze Ancillary Tests - Biopsies:** Initial CT-guided biopsies were inconclusive, highlighting diagnostic challenges. Surgical pathology from resection indicated "atypical epithelioid pathology" with IHC positivity for D2-40, calretinin, and WT1. These markers are typically associated with mesothelioma, reinforcing the initial MPM suspicion based on pathology at that time. However, the *follow-up* pathological workup is the key, revealing a "rare STK11 adnexal tumor" with associated mutations. This completely changes the diagnostic landscape.

<step 5> **Interpret Ancillary Tests - Genetic Testing:** Negative germline mutations for Peutz-Jeghers Syndrome are reported. While STK11 mutations are associated with Peutz-Jeghers, the negative germline test suggests the STK11 mutation in this case is likely somatic (tumor-specific) or related to a different, non-Peutz-Jeghers associated STK11-mutated tumor type. The presence of ARID1A and EP300 mutations further points towards a complex tumor biology and is consistent with mutations found in various cancer types.

<step 6> **Synthesize and Re-evaluate the Diagnosis:**  The initial diagnosis of MPM, while understandable based on initial IHC markers, is superseded by the definitive finding of a "rare STK11 adnexal tumor" from the follow-up pathological workup. The patient's symptoms and disease progression are now explained by this new diagnosis. The initial treatment for MPM may have had a temporary effect or the disease course was slow, but ultimately, the true underlying pathology is the STK11 mutated adnexal tumor. Adnexal tumors arise from skin appendages and can metastasize internally, explaining the liver lesions and metastatic spread.

<step 7> **Formulate the Final Diagnosis:** The final diagnosis is based on the most recent and definitive pathological and genetic findings, which is the "STK11 adnexal tumor". Given the imaging findings of additional lesions suggestive of metastasis, the disease is likely metastatic.

### Answer:
STK11 mutated adnexal tumor, metastatic.
